---
title: "Gridded population nowcasting methodology for the Gaza Strip using telecommunications data"
author: Douglas R. Leasure\footnote{Senior Researcher and Data Scientist, Demographic Science Unit, Nuffield Department of Population Health, University of Oxford and Oxford University Innovation}  and Firas Qaimary\footnote{A/Head Information Management Unit, Occupied Palestinian Territory, United Nations Office for Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs}
date: "15 April 2025"
format: 
    pdf:
        number-sections: false
        toc: false
        toc-depth: 3
        latex-engine: xelatex
fontfamily: roboto
fontsize: 12pt
geometry: a4paper
output: pdf_document
---

**Classification:** Strictly Confidential\footnote{See UNOCHA (2024) Information Sharing Protocol for the Occupied Palestinian Territory, available from https://www.unocha.org/publications/report/occupied-palestinian-territory/information-sharing-protocol-occupied-palestinian-territory-june-2024}  

**Sensitivity:** Severe Sensitivity  

**Suggested Citation:** Leasure DR and Qaimary F. 2025. Gridded population nowcasting methodology for the Gaza Strip using telecommunications data. Oxford University Innovation. April 15, 2025.

\vfill
*While every effort was given to produce data suitable for operational purposes, the results are provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. In no event shall the authors or copyright holders be liable for any claim, damages or other liability arising from, out of or in connection with the contents of this report, supplementary data, or source code.*

\newpage

\hypersetup{linkcolor=black}
\setcounter{tocdepth}{3}
\tableofcontents


```{r}
#| include: false

options(scipen = 999)

# libraries
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

#---- USER OPTIONS ----#
pop_date <- as.Date("2025-04-10")
#----------------------#

# directories
data_dir <- file.path("wd", "out", "telecom_towers_deterministic", "results", paste0("Oxford_Gaza_NowPop_", pop_date))

if (!dir.exists(data_dir)) {
  data_dir <- file.path("..", data_dir)
}

# load data
pop_gov <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, "all_dates", "pop_gov_all_dates.csv")) |> filter(date == pop_date)
pop_mun <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, "all_dates", "pop_mun_all_dates.csv")) |> filter(date == pop_date)
pop_nbr <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, "all_dates", "pop_nbr_all_dates.csv")) |> filter(date == pop_date)
```


\newpage
# Executive Summary

The University of Oxford is working with Acted and the Site Management Cluster for Gaza as well as the interagency AIM working group led by UNOCHA to provide support for real-time demographic estimation in the Gaza strip. This report describes methodology developed for demographic estimation using telecommunications data from April 9-10 to produce population estimates on a 100 m grid across Gaza. The population estimates are summarised at neighbourhood, municipality, and governorate levels in the [Findings](#findings) section of the report. 

The methodology was based on tower-level counts of idle subscribers from two telecommunications networks in Gaza. Other input data included reference population data from the Ministry of Social Development, previous population estimates based on polio vaccinations, evacuation orders compiled by UNOCHA, and remotely-sensed building footprints. See the the [Methodology](#methodology) section for details of the input data and the analysis.

The [Assumptions and Limitations](#assumptions-and-limitations) section details important caveats that should be considered when making operational decisions from these data. 

[Recommendations](#recommendations-and-conclusion) are provided regarding next steps and considerations for future work which will include updates to these telecommunication-based population estimates as new data become available.

\newpage
# Background

The protracted conflict in the Gaza Strip following the Hamas attack on Israel and subsequent Israeli invasion into Gaza resulted in the forced displacement of nearly 2 million people in the Gaza Strip and widespread destruction of infrastructure in every governorate. Oxford University has engaged with the humanitarian response since the beginning of the crisis through the interagency AIM working group to provide real-time demographic estimation, initially relying on digital trace data from social media usage to estimate population sizes in each governorate on a daily basis. This data stream was lost on May 15, 2024 due to a global change in how Meta provides information about user activity on Facebook and Instagram (the two primary data sources). This report is the fourth in a series of reports on population estimation for Gaza using other data sources since that time. 

Following the detection of polio virus in the Gaza strip in July 2024, the World Health Organization and international partners negotiated access for a polio vaccination campaign to contain the outbreak. The first three reports developed a bespoke capture-recapture methodology for demographic estimation using data from three rounds of the polio vaccination campaign from September 2024 to February 2025. Those reports provided estimates of population sizes (children under 10 and total population) along with vaccination coverage for Gaza governorates and municipalities (Leasure 2025a, 2025b, 2025c). The fourth round of the polio vaccination campataign for Gaza was scheduled for April 2, 2025 but has been delayed indefinitely due to increased hostilities on the ground. 

OUI has partnered with UNOCHA to securely analyse network data from two telecommunications networks in Gaza. The current report describes methodologies developed for population estimation using these data and reports the results from analysing data for April 9-10, 2025.

# Objectives

- Estimate population sizes for each 100 m grid cell inside the Gaza Strip. 
- Integrate counts of idle subscribers connected to individual towers across two mobile network providers.
- Align the spatial distribution of population estimates with evacuation orders and built-up areas.
- Summarise gridded population estimates within neighbourhoods, municipalities, and governorates.


# Methodology

## Data

Telecommunications data were provided by two mobile network operators in Gaza. Both datasets included counts of idle subscribers on each tower for a given date along with GPS coordinates of each tower. Dates included in this analysis were from April 9 for one provider and April 10, 2025 for the other. There were no individual identifiers included to identify individual subscribers associated with more than one tower.

Two datasets were used as a population reference from a previous date. The Ministry of Social Development provided a population registry that was collected via digital means on February 25 for the North Gaza and Gaza governorates. Population estimates based on polio-vaccination data from February 22 - 26 were used as population reference data for Rafah, Khan Younis, and Deir al Balah governorates.  

Evacuation orders were obtained from a UNOCHA dashboard (UNOCHA 2025) and the GIS shapefile defining the spatial geometries of evacuation blocks was obtained from the "gaza-geojson" GitHub repository (bothness 2025). Evacuation orders in the UNOCHA dashboard are taken from X account of AvichayAdraee. Spatial geometries of various buffer zones that have been established along the border of the Gaza strip on different dates were provided by UNOCHA (personal communication).

Building footprints for Gaza were obtained from Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team (HOT 2025). These building footprints represent pre-conflict conditions. They were the result of a mapping effort to improve OpenStreetMap building footprints that was undertaken in 2024 to add 59,223 buildings in Gaza (that were previously missing from OpenStreetMaps) and to improve the geometries of thousands of existing building foorprints. 

Administrative boundaries were provided by UNOCHA (personal communication) including harmonised boundaries for Gaza governorates, municipalities, and neighbourhoods with names and p-codes. 

## Analysis

The goal of the analysis was to estimate the population size on April 10th for every 100 m grid cell within the Gaza strip based on the number of idle subscribers on individual geolocated cell towers for two mobile network operators. This included estimating the effective coverage area around each tower, distributing the subscriber count across 100 m grid cells in the coverage area, combining subscriber grids across towers and mobile network operators, adjusting for mobile penetration rates, and constraining the population total to the estimated total population of the Gaza strip. 

The effective tower radius around each was estimated based on a logistic function of declinging coverage rates with increasing distance from the tower: 

\begin{align*}
  p_{t,i} &= \frac{1 + e^{-\kappa r_t}}{1 + e^{\kappa d_{t,i} - \kappa r_t}}
\end{align*}

\noindent where $p_{t,i}$ is the probability that a subscriber on the network is detected on tower $t$ from grid cell $i$ based on the distance $d{_t,i}$ between the grid cell and the tower and the coverage radius $r_t$ of the tower (@fig-detection). $\kappa$ defines the rate of decline in the detection probability as distance increases beyond the coverage radius, and it was set to 2 for all towers. Detection probabilities summed to one across all grid cells for each tower. 

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-detection

r <- 1
kappa <- 2
d <- seq(0, 3, length = 100)

p <- (1 + exp(-kappa * r)) / (1 + exp(kappa * (d - r)))

plot(
  x = d,
  y = p,
  type = "l",
  ylab = "detection probability (p)",
  xlab = "distance (d)"
)
abline(
  v = r,
  lty = 2
)
```

The number of subscribers detected on each tower was multiplied by the grid of detection probabilities to redistribute subscriber counts from individual towers across 100 m grid cells in the coverage area surrounding the tower. A weighted mean was taken across all towers for each grid cell to obtain an estimate of the number of subscribers on each network in each grid cell, using detection probabilities $p_{t,i}$ as the weights. A weighted mean was used rather than summing subscribers across towers to acknowledges that individual subscribers from a network could be detected on multiple towers. This procedure produced counts of subscribers on each network for each grid cell. These were then summed to produce a subscriber grid with the total number of subscribers in each grid cell across the two networks for which we had data. 

The next step was to extrapolate the subscriber grid to estimate total population including people not detectable on either of the mobile networks. To achieve this, penetration rates (i.e. the proportion of the population detectable across the two mobile networks) were calculated at the governorate level by linking a subscriber grid from February 20, 2025 to reference population data that was available for that date. The reference population data for North Gaza and Gaza governorates were from a population registry administered by the Palestinian Ministry of Social Development, and the population data for Deir al Balah, Khan Youis, and Rafah governorates were population estimates based on polio vaccination data (Leasure 2025c). These penetration rates were applied to the subscriber grid for April 10, 2025 to estimate population sizes. A final post hoc adjustment was applied to ensure that the total population estimate across all grid cells for the Gaza Strip summed to 2.1 million, in alignment with the latest estimate from the Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics. 

# Findings

Please note that supplementary data are included with this report that are described in [Appendix A](#appendix-a).

The primary output of the analysis is a 100-m gridded population estimate with full coverage of the Gaza strip (@fig-pop-grid). The gridded estimates are intended to provide flexibility to summarise population estimates for any spatial units (e.g. administrative units, health catchment areas). 

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-pop-grid
#| fig-cap: "Population estimates for every 100-m grid cell."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-align: center
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(data_dir, "supplementary_data", paste0("fig_pop_grid_", pop_date, ".jpg")))
```

Aggregated population estimates within larger spatial units are expected to have the best accuracy (@fig-pop-nbr), whereas the gridded estimates likely do not have a high degree of accuracy for every 100-m grid cell. Aggregated population estimates are provided for governorates, municipalities, and neighbourhoods in the sub-sections that follow.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-pop-nbr
#| fig-cap: "Gridded population estimates aggregated to neighborhoods."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-align: center
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(data_dir, "supplementary_data", paste0("fig_pop_nbr_", pop_date, ".jpg")))
```

## Governorate-level

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: tbl-pop-gov

pop_gov |>
  arrange(ADM2_PCODE) |>
  select(ADM2_EN, population) |>
  rename(
    Governorate = ADM2_EN,
    Population = population
  ) |>
  mutate(Population = prettyNum(round(Population), big.mark = ",")) |>
  kable(
    caption = paste0("Population estimates for each governorate on ", format(pop_date, "%d %B %Y"), "."),
    booktabs = TRUE
  )
```


## Municipality-level

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: tbl-pop-mun

pop_mun |>
  arrange(ADM2_PCODE, ADM3_PCODE) |>
  select(ADM3_EN, ADM2_EN, population) |>
  rename(
    Governorate = ADM2_EN,
    Municipality = ADM3_EN,
    Population = population
  ) |>
  mutate(
    Population = prettyNum(round(Population), big.mark = ",")
  ) |>
  kable(
    caption = paste0("Population estimates for each municipality on ", format(pop_date, "%d %B %Y"), "."),
    booktabs = TRUE
  )
```


## Neighbourhood-level

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: tbl-pop-nbr

pop_nbr |>
  arrange(ADM2_PCODE, ADM3_PCODE, ADM4_PCODE) |>
  select(ADM4_EN, ADM3_EN, ADM2_EN, population) |>
  rename(
    Governorate = ADM2_EN,
    Municipality = ADM3_EN,
    Neighbourhood = ADM4_EN,
    Population = population
  ) |>
  mutate(
    Population = prettyNum(round(Population), big.mark = ","),
    Municipality = case_when(
      nchar(Municipality) > 12 ~ paste0(substr(Municipality, 1, 12), "..."),
      TRUE ~ Municipality
    )
  ) |>
  kable(
    caption = paste0("Population estimates for each neighbourhood on ", format(pop_date, "%d %B %Y"), "."),
    booktabs = TRUE
  )
```


# Assumptions and Limitations

- The methodology used here cannot produce robust estimates of uncertainty in the population estimates, so confidence intervals cannot be provided.
- The relative mobile penetration rates among governorates were assumed to be constant through time. Population estimates from this approach are robust to increases or descreases in mobile penetration that occur at the same rate across governorates, but would be sensitive to uneven changes in penetration rates across governorates. Penetration rates are assumed to be homogenous within each governorate.
- Evacuation orders were assumed to result in total evacuation for a 48 hour period. Population estimates were forced to zero for evacuation zones beginning on the day of evacuation orders and then telecom-based population estimation resumed 48-hours later.
- Evacuation buffer zones along the border of the Gaza strip were assumed to be totally evacuated.
- The coverage area of towers were assumed to be proportional to the proximity of nearby towers. If real coverage areas for individual towers did not align with this assumption, then the spatial allocation of subscriber counts from those towers would be inaccurate. This could impact population estimates slightly in areas of overlap between multiple towers.
- The entire population was assumed to be within the coverage area of at least one tower. Any populations that were entirely outside the coverage area of the mobile network towers would be allocated to an area with mobile coverage. This would cause inaccuracies in the spatial allocation of population, but should not strongly affect population estimates aggregated to larger administrative areas. 

# Recommendations and Conclusion

The tower-level telecommunications data from two mobile network operators in the Gaza strip provide a much needed source of information on the recent spatial distribution of populations. The method implemented here carries a number of assumptions and limitations, but produced population estimates suitable for operational uses. 

The next steps will be to regularly update the population estimates as new telecommunications become available, and to piece together a historical time series of population changes using previous telecommunications dating to January 2025. 

A hierarchical Bayesian statistical model was prototyped based on the rapid-response methodology presented in this report. This more advanced methodology has several challenges that need to be overcome before the approach could produce reliable population estimates. The statistical approach would provide a few advantages including quantifying uncertainty of population estimates, facilitating direct integration of multiple data sources in a single model despite temporal misalignment (e.g. polio vaccinations and telecommunications), and integration of spatial covariates like remotely-sensed building footprints, building damage, and informal settlements (i.e. tents). Development of the statistical approach will continue as a secondary priority to producing rapid-response population estimates in near real-time for operational purposes using the existing methodology.

# References

bothness. 2025. gaza-geojson. Accessed 1 April 2025. https://github.com/bothness/gaza-geojson

HOT [Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team]. 2025. Gaza building footprints pre-conflict, update 2024. Accessed April 1, 2025. https://data.humdata.org/dataset/hotosm_pse_buildings?

Leasure DR. 2025a. Mark-recapture population estimation for Gaza using polio vaccination data: Round 1 of vaccination campaign (Sep 2024). Oxford University Innovation. January 17, 2025.

Leasure DR. 2025b. Mark-recapture population estimation for Gaza using polio vaccination data: Round 2 of vaccination campaign (Oct-Nov 2024). Oxford University Innovation. January 28, 2025.

Leasure DR. 2025c. Mark-recapture population estimation for municipalities of the Gaza Strip using polio vaccination data: Round 3 of vaccination campaign (Feb 22-26, 2025). Oxford University Innovation. March 26, 2025.

UNOCHA [United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs]. 2025. Gaza Strip - OPT: Israeli military evacuation orders. Accessed April 11, 2025. https://app.powerbi.com/view?r=eyJrIjoiNjY4NzkwMjItYzI2MC00NmQ4LWE2MTktMDQxMjBmMTk5NmIwIiwidCI6IjBmOWUzNWRiLTU0NGYtNGY2MC1iZGNjLTVlYTQxNmU2ZGM3MCIsImMiOjh9. 

\newpage
# Appendix A

## Supplementary Data

The following supplementary data accompany this report and are available upon request to UNOCHA through the Assessment and Information Management (AIM) Working Group:  

1. pop_grid_[date].tif - 100-m gridded population estimates in geotiff format.  
2. pop_nbr_[date].gpkg - Neighbourhood-level population estimates in geopackage format to include spatial geometries defining neighbourhood boundaries.  
3. pop_nbr_[date].csv - Neighbourhood-level population estimates (@tbl-pop-nbr) in comma-separated-values format.  
4. pop_mun_[date].gpkg - Municipality-level population estimates in geopackage format to include spatial geometries defining municipality boundaries.  
5. pop_mun_[date].csv - Municipality-level population estimates (@tbl-pop-mun) in comma-separated-values format.  
6. pop_gov_[date].gpkg - Governorate-level population estimates in geopackage format to include spatial geometries defining governorate boundaries.  
7. pop_gov_[date].csv - Governorate-level population estimates (@tbl-pop-gov) in comma-separated-values format.  
8. fig_pop_grid_[date].jpg - A map of gridded population estimates (@fig-pop-grid) in JPEG format.  
9. fig_pop_nbr_[date].jpg - A map of neighborhood-level population estimates (@fig-pop-nbr) in JPEG format.
